ground_truth.title: How I'd learn to be a web designer if I could do it all over again — Paige Brunton
ground_truth.author: Paige Brunton
ground_truth.main_content:
Considering a career in web design? It's the best job in the world! But when I began as a web designer back in 2016, I made a tonne of mistakes and would do totally differently if I was doing it all again - here's what you need to know if you're learning to be a web designer today.

What I wish someone had told me is that most people spend an absolute TON of time learning to code, but that's kind of a waste of time these days as it's irrelevant to most web designers. Website building used to need this - but now it's so much more accessible, and a lot more about strategy and design than code! Stop wasting time learning HTML because if you custom code a site, it's going to look like it was built in 2003.

The real first step to becoming a designer these days is to research and pick one web design platform to learn. It's kind of like when people say they want to learn to play sports and then go and learn golf, hockey and swimming. They're three totally different things, and while there's some complimentary skills from learning all three, you're going to get better at golf a lot faster by just learning golf, and not also hockey and swimming at the same time.

For educational resources, books go out of date before you can blink because web design changes so quickly. Same thing goes for learning web design or coding at university or college. They're teaching the tech and coding of 5 years ago, not what's being used today. Do your research online, find a teacher you resonate with, binge their content online, read all their blogs and YouTube videos and find someone who doesn't bore you to tears while they teach!

Create a portfolio while practicing. If you want to be a freelance designer and get real clients, you'll need a portfolio. But even more than this, you're going to need the confidence you can actually build websites. Normally a client will send you the details of their business and the site they want, called a client brief, basically a short description of what they want, the vibe and their inspiration websites.

Before you can actually take on clients, you need to figure out what to charge! A few hundred dollars? A few thousand? There are designers charging $10k per project - how do you command those prices?
